---
output: 
  phstemplates::phs_report_docx:
    reference_docx: "phs-offdev-report.docx"
    fig_caption: no
    cover_page: "phs-offdev-cover.docx"
    cover_title: "My title"
    cover_subtitle: "My subtitle"
    cover_date: "DD MM YYYY"
    toc_depth: 3
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}

# Psychological Therapies

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, dpi = 300)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(flextable)
library(officer)
library(magrittr)
library(arrow)
library(tidyverse)

# define dataset selector variable
dataset_choice <- "PT"

# source data for tables and inline values
source("/PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/scripts/luke/CAPTND/07_publication/script/functions/source_data_for_markdown.R", local = knitr::knit_global())
```


# Introduction

This publication of statistics in development presents information relating to 
appointment activity in Psychological Therapies (PT) from the Child, Adolescent, 
and Psychological Therapies National Dataset (CAPTND), at NHS board and Scotland-level.
Presented in this report are data on appointment Did Not Attend (DNA) rates amongst 
PT patients relating to the period between April 2025 and June 2026. Total appointment
and first contact appointment DNA rates are presented by population group to try and
better understand patterns of non-attendance across different demographics, and 
determine whether differences in DNA rates represent inequalities in access to PT
services across NHS Scotland. Here ‘Scotland-level’ refers to the aggregated regional
NHS board figures that are available in CAPTND. 

CAPTND is a dataset in development that collates information for mental health 
services at patient-level and records each individual’s journey through the service. 
CAPTND data are still under development therefore any inferences or 
conclusions from this analysis must be treated with caution, and should not be compared 
to the [official aggregate PT collection and waiting times](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/psychological-therapies-waiting-times/psychological-therapies-waiting-times-quarter-ending-september-2026/).    

## Data Quality

Good quality data are crucial for accuracy and interpretation, and Public Health 
Scotland is working with all NHS boards to implement robust data collection for 
CAPTND. Some data items, namely ‘UCPN’, ‘UPI’, and ‘CHI’, are required to create 
a unique and complete care pathway for an individual within CAPTND (see 
[Variables Reported](#variables-reported) section below). For records where these
key variables are not available the pathway cannot be created accurately and
cannot therefore be used in CAPTND analysis. A comparison of referral counts before
and after pathway creation can be found in the accompanying [data quality documents](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/psychological-therapies-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-3-march-2026/) 

Data returns for CAPTND began in June 2019, and following extensive data quality 
checking and collaboration with NHS boards data quality improved sufficiently by 
January 2020 to enable initial and limited reporting to commence.
Improving data quality in CAPTND is an ongoing process, meaning data from previous
quarters may not be comparable to the current reporting period.   

As the development of CAPTND progresses, greater detail on individuals’ journeys 
within PT will be reported.  

## Variables Reported

See the [Glossary](#glossary) for definitions of terminology and derived values.  

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Unique Care Pathway Number (UCPN):** An  identifier allocated to new referrals 
to a consultant-led, nurse-led, or other service, to enable identification of
patient pathways. These are only unique *within* NHS boards. 
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Community Health Index (CHI):** The [Community 
Health Index (CHI)](https://www.datadictionary.nhs.uk/attributes/community_health_index_number.html)
is a population register, which is used in Scotland for health care purposes. 
The CHI number uniquely identifies a person on the index, the first six digits 
of the CHI number relate to the person's date of birth (DD MM YY).
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Unique Patient Identifier (UPI):** The UPI is a locally-generated code used to
uniquely identify a patient in cases where the CHI number is not available.
</Div>
 
<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Sex:** Biological sex as recorded at birth. 'Not known' refers to indeterminate
sex at birth, including intersex. The use of 'Not specified' refers to instances
where the information is not available.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Age group:** Age in years is calculated at the point of referral from reported date 
of birth. When date of birth is not reported, the first six digits of the CHI 
number are used. Age is then grouped into 5-year age categories for reporting purposes.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**SIMD quintile:** [Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation (SIMD)](https://www.gov.scot/collections/scottish-index-of-multiple-deprivation-2020/) 
is a postcode-derived measure of relative deprivation. If an area is identified as 
‘deprived’, this can relate to residents having a low income, but it can also 
mean fewer resources or opportunities. SIMD looks at the extent to which an area 
is deprived across seven domains: income, employment, education, health, access 
to services, crime and housing. Residence in a deprived postcode area does not 
necessarily mean an individual themselves experiences deprivation. Not all patients 
are treated in their NHS board of residence. Please note that NHS board areas may
not have all five quintiles represented.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Urban rural classification:** [Scottish Government Urban Rural Classification 2022](https://www.gov.scot/publications/scottish-government-urban-rural-classification-2022/pages/introduction/) 
provides a standardised approach to defining urban and rural areas, and was developed 
to improve understanding of issues facing urban, rural and remote communities in Scotland.
The classification is based on population, defined by the National Records of Scotland, 
and accessibility, based on drive time analysis to categorise accessible and 
remote areas of Scotland. The urban rural classification is available in multiple forms, 
but the 8-fold classification has been adopted for this analysis to provide greater 
granularity in comparisons between remote regions. The urban rural class applied to 
each patient is based on the postcode of their most recent referral.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral received date / Referral date:** The date on which a health or social 
care service receives a referral. If this cannot be provided, ‘Referral date’ is 
used instead as these dates are often very similar. If neither of these fields are
provided the individual’s record cannot be counted as it cannot be placed within 
the time frame reported.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral accepted:** A categorical variable stating whether an individual's
referral has been accepted, not accepted, decision pending, or the information is missing.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral non-acceptance reason:** The reason why a referral was not accepted 
by the service provider. 
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral non-acceptance action:** The action taken following an initial referral 
not being accepted into the service. Please note that these are data under development
and may not be recorded in all NHS boards.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Appointment date:** The date on which an appointment occurred or was planned 
to occur. The first appointment date in an individual's pathway is referred to 
as the **first contact appointment.**
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Appointment attendance status:** Indicates whether the patient attended their 
appointment and provides context for non-attendance. This is used to calculate
Did Not Attend (DNA) rates.
</Div>


\newpage

# Main Points

For PT data recorded in CAPTND across NHS Scotland, in the publication period 
ending `r date_label`:

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1">

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1">

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1">

</Div>

\newpage

# Results and Commentary
## PT total appointment DNAs
Across NHS Scotland, there were a total of **586,353** appointments recorded in CAPTND 
for the publication period between April 2025 and June 2026. Data broken down by 
quarters can be seen in the accompanying [data files](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/psychological-therapies-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-1-september-2026/).     
    
The Did Not Attend (DNA) rate is an important measure to consider, as DNA appointments 
are difficult to fill due to the unplanned nature of the non-attendance 
(see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information). 
Of the **586,353** total appointments for the 15-month publication period ending June 2026, 
the overall DNA rate was **9.6%** (**56,228**) appointments not attended). 
This represents a significant amount of lost capacity within PT services, potentially 
impacting the length of time patients wait for treatment.   
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Table 1: PT total appointments and total DNA rate by health board, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>
```{r Total appt DNA table, ft.align = "left"}

# Set up some formatting for flextable to be applied to most tables
my_ft_format <- function(ft) {
  ft %>%
    flextable::bold(part = "header") %>%
    bg(bg = "#43358B", part = "header") %>%
    color(color = "white", part = "header") %>%
    align(align = "left", part = "header") %>%
    valign(valign = "center", part = "header") %>%
    valign(valign = "top", part = "body") %>%
    colformat_num(big.mark = "") %>%
    font(fontname = "arial", part = "all") %>%
    fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") %>%
    border(border = fp_border_default(color = "#000000", width = 0.5),
           part = "all")
}

flextable(table_data7, theme_fun = NULL) %>%
  my_ft_format() %>%
  flextable::bold(i = nrow(table_data7)) %>%
  align(j = 2:4, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
  height(height = 0.236, part = "body") %>%
  hrule(rule = "atleast", part = "body") %>%
  width(j = 1, width = 4, unit = "cm") %>%
  width(j = 2:4, width = 3, unit = "cm")
  #set_caption(caption = paste0("Quarter ending ", date_label), align_with_table = TRUE)

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. \ \ \ CAPTND extract `r data_analysis_latest_date`
    
<br>        
    
### PT total DNA rate by age group and sex
In PT, total appointment DNA rates for males were highest amongst the '25-39' year age group 
in NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026, at **11.9%**. For females in the 
same period, first contact DNA rates were highest amongst the 'Under 25' age group 
at **10.3%**. Males were more likely to DNA an appointment than females across all 
age groups, except in the '65 plus' age group over the 15-month publication period.   
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by aggregated age group and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_age_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_age_sex_PT.png"))

```
    
### PT total DNA rate by age group and appointment month
Total PT appointment DNA rates by aggregated age group appear very consistent across
appointment months for the period between April 2025 and June 2026, with little 
indication of seasonal variation in non-attendance rates. DNA rates across NHS 
Scotland were noticeable lower amongst the '65 plus' age group, with appointment
non-attendance recorded at around 5.0% over the 15-month publication period. DNA
rates were highest amongst the 'Under 25' and '25-39' age groups, with rates
consistently recorded at or just above 10.0% across NHS Scotland.    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by aggregated age group and appointment month, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_age_mth, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/dna_agg_age_tt_PT.png"))

```   
    
### PT total DNA rate by SIMD and sex
Deprivation based on SIMD quintile appears to be closely associated with total PT 
appointment DNA rates across NHS Scotland. Between April 2025 and June 2026, females 
from the most deprived areas (**12.1%**) were more likely to DNA a PT appointment than 
those from the least deprived areas (**6.9%**). A similar trend was observed amongst male 
patients, with a DNA rate of **12.1%** recorded amongst those from the most deprived SIMD 
quintile compared to **7.5%** in the least deprived quintile across NHS Scotland. DNA 
rates for both males and females were above the corresponding mean in both SIMD 1 
(most deprived) and SIMD 2 for the publication period.    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by SIMD quintile and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_simd_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_simd_sex_PT.png"))

```

### PT total DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex
Total PT appointment DNA rates for both males (**13.2%**) and females (**11.7%**) were 
highest amongst patients residing in 'very remote small towns' across NHS Scotland
based on the 8-fold urban rural classification (Figure). Amongst females, total
appointment DNA rates were lowest for patients residing in 'remote small towns'
(**7.1%**) between April 2025 and June 2026, and for males were lowest amongst patients
living in 'remote rural' (**8.8%**) settings. Given the differences in accessibility,
it is worth noting that total appointment DNA rates amongst patients residing in
remote or very remote rural areas were either similar to or below the rates recorded
in large and other urban areas across NHS Scotland. This could, however, be explained
by differences in factors such as relative deprivation, and the location of planned
appointments, including the utilisation of digital technologies to deliver therapy
to more remote, rural settings. 
For definitions of each rurality class, please consult the Scottish Government’s 
overview [documentation](https://www.gov.scot/publications/scottish-government-urban-rural-classification-2022/pages/overview/).   
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_ur_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_ur_sex_PT.png"))

```

### PT total DNA rate by appointment location and sex
The location of the planned PT appointment will vary based on the needs of the 
patient and is likely to influence non-attendance rates. Figure x shows the total
appointment DNA rate by the location of the planned appointment and the patient’s
sex reported at referral. The ten most common appointment locations recorded across
NHS Scotland for the period between April 2025 and June 2026 are displayed in
descending order from top to bottom.  
    
Amongst both males and females, total appointment DNA rates were highest across 
NHS Scotland when the planned appointment location was recorded as 'health centre' 
(**12.9%** male; **12.7%** female). Total appointment DNA rates were lowest for both sexes 
when the planned appointment location was recorded as 'ward' (**3.7%** male; **4.2%** female) 
or the 'patients home' (**3.1%** male; **1.7%** female). The difference in total 
appointment DNA rates between males and females was typically quite small between 
planned appointment locations. However, when the planned appointment location was 
recorded as 'NHS Near Me', males had a DNA rate of **12.7%**, compared to **10.2%** amongst 
females in NHS Scotland – a difference of 2.5 percentage points. NHS Near Me is a service that allows 
patients to attend appointments using video call, rather than attending in person. 
It is, therefore, interesting to note that males across NHS Scotland were noticeably 
more likely to DNA these digital appointments compared to females over the 15-month 
publication period.    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by location of planned appointment and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_loc_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_loc_sex_PT.png"))

```

### PT total DNA rate by appointment location for least deprived SIMD quintile
In the least deprived SIMD quintile, there were a total of **88,002** appointments 
offered across NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026. Figure x shows the
total number of appointments and the DNA rate by planned appointment location for
patients living in the least deprived SIMD quintile based on postcode at the time
of referral. The top ten most common appointment locations recorded for patients
living in the least deprived SIMD quintile across NHS Scotland are shown in 
ascending from top to bottom.   
    
'Out-patient clinic' was the most commonly recorded appointment location over the
period making up **62.2%** (**54,731**) of the total PT appointments offered, with an 
associated DNA rate of **7.2%** for the publication period. The DNA rate was below 
**9.0%** for all planned appointment locations with the exception of the 'health centre',
which had a DNA rate of **11.2%** across SIMD quintile 5 in NHS Scotland.      
        
In the least deprived quintile, appointments that made use of digital technologies, 
recorded as 'telephone consultations', 'NHS Near Me' or 'attend anywhere' made up
a total of **29.8%** (**26,263**) of all offered appointments. The DNA rate for these 
appointment types varied from **4.4%** for 'telephone consultations' to **8.7%** for
'NHS Near Me' over the 15-month publication period in NHS Scotland.       
        
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointments and crude DNA rate for top 10 most common planned appointment locations for patients living in the least deprived SIMD quintile, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_loc_simd5_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_loc_simd5_PT.png"))

```

### PT total DNA rate by appointment location for most deprived SIMD quintile

In the most deprived SIMD quintile, there were a total of **122,643** appointments 
offered across NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026. Shown in Figure x 
is the total number of appointments offered and the DNA rate by planned appointment
location for patients living the in the most deprived SIMD quintile based on 
postcode at the time of referral. The top ten most common appointment locations
recorded for patients living in the most deprived SIMD quintile across NHS Scotland
are displayed in ascending from top to bottom.    
    
'Out-patient clinic' was the most commonly recorded appointment location over the
publication period making up **54.8%** (**67,192**) of the total PT appointments offered,
with an associated DNA rate of **14.0%** for the publication period – almost twice 
as high as the DNA rate in the least deprived quintile.       
    
In the most deprived quintile, 'telephone consultations', 'NHS Near Me' and 'attend anywhere'
appointments made up a total of **15.8%** (**19,321**) of all offered appointments. This suggests
that in the most deprived quintile, appointments utilising digital technologies 
made up a much lower proportion of total appointments offered compared to the least
deprived quintile over the same period. The DNA rate for these appointment types
varied from **7.6%** for 'telephone consultations' to **15.0%** for 'NHS Near Me' over 
the 15-month publication period in NHS Scotland - notably higher than the 
corresponding rates for patients from the least deprived SIMD quintile.   
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointments and crude DNA rate for top 10 most common planned appointment locations for patients living in the most deprived SIMD quintile, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_loc_simd1_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_loc_simd1_PT.png"))

```

### PT total DNA rate by geographic region
The total PT appointment DNA rate appears to be relatively consistent across health 
board regions for the 15-month publication period, which is likely due to the 
improving data quality in CAPTND. For PT, total appointment DNA rates appear highest 
across the North and West regions throughout the publication period, with both having 
a total appointment DNA rate fluctuating around the 10.0% mark. The East region recorded 
the lowest total appointment DNA rate in almost every month between April 2025 and June 2026, 
with the rate consistently below 10.0% (Figure ) 
(see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information). 

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by health board region, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_hb_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/dna_hb_region_PT.png"))

```

### PT total DNA rate by weekday of planned appointment
There was little variation between the weekday of the planned PT appointment and
the DNA rate across NHS Scotland for the publication period (Figure ). Total 
appointment DNA rates were, however, marginally higher on a Monday for both males
(**10.2%**) and females (**9.8%**) compared to all other weekdays between April 2025 
and June 2026.    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by weekday of planned appointment, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_weekday_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_weekday_sex_PT.png"))

```
       
    
## PT first contact appointment DNAs

Of the **586,353** total appointments in PT across NHS Scotland in the
latest quarter, **12.6%** (**74,077**) were first contact appointments (Table 2). 
The first contact appointment is the first appointment date a patient is offered 
in their treatment journey (see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information).   

The first contact DNA rate is represented as the proportion of all planned first
contact appointments which were not attended. DNAs in first contact appointments
represent a delay to treatment starting. The PT first contact DNA rate across NHS Scotland in the
latest quarter was **9.3%**, representing **6,897** of **74,077** first contact appointments.  

\newpage

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Table 2: PT first contact appointments and first contact DNA rate by health board, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>

```{r Appointments table, ft.align = "left"}

flextable(table_data8, theme_fun = NULL) %>%
  my_ft_format() %>%
  flextable::bold(i = nrow(table_data8)) %>%
  align(j = 2:5, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
  height(height = 0.236, part = "body") %>%
  hrule(rule = "atleast", part = "body") %>%
  width(j = 1, width = 4, unit = "cm") %>%
  width(j = 2:3, width = 3, unit = "cm")  %>%
  width(j = 4:5, width = 2.5, unit = "cm")
  #set_caption(caption = paste0("Quarter ending ", date_label), align_with_table = TRUE)

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. \ \ \ CAPTND extract `r data_analysis_latest_date`
    
<br> 

### PT first contact DNA rate by age group and sex
In PT, first contact DNA rates were highest for both males and females amongst the
'25-39' year age group in NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026 at **12.4%** 
and **9.7%** respectively. Males were more likely to DNA a first contact appointment 
than females across all age groups, except in the '65 plus' age group over the 
15-month publication period.

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by aggregated age group and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_age_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_age_sex_PT.png"))

```
    
### PT first contact DNA rate by SIMD and sex
Deprivation based on SIMD quintile appears to be closely associated with PT first contact 
appointment DNA rates across NHS Scotland. Between April 2025 and June 2026, females 
from the most deprived areas (**12.2%**) were more likely to DNA a first contact PT appointment
than those from the least deprived areas (**6.1%**). A similar trend was observed amongst 
male patients, with a first contact DNA rate of **14.4%** recorded amongst those from the 
most deprived SIMD quintile compared to **6.8%** in the least deprived quintile across NHS 
Scotland. DNA rates for both males and females were above the corresponding mean in 
both SIMD 1 (most deprived) and SIMD 2 for the publication period.

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by SIMD quintile and sex reported, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_simd_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_simd_sex_PT.png"))

```
    
### PT first contact DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex
In PT, first contact appointment DNA rates for both males (**15.0%**) and females 
(**13.2%**) were highest amongst patients residing in 'very remote small towns' 
across NHS Scotland based on the 8-fold urban rural classification (Figure ). 
Amongst females, first contact appointment DNA rates between April 2025 and June
2026 were lowest for patients residing in 'remote rural' settings (**7.3%**), and 
for males were lowest amongst patients living in 'accessible rural' settings (**8.3%**). 
For definitions of each rurality class, please consult the Scottish Government’s 
overview [documentation](https://www.gov.scot/publications/scottish-government-urban-rural-classification-2022/pages/overview/)    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_by_ur_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_ur_sex_PT.png"))

```    
    
### PT first contact DNA rate by length of wait

The length of time between the referral received date and the first scheduled PT 
appointment date appears to be associated with the rate of non-attendance amongst 
patients. In NHS Scotland, patients that had waited between 0 to 18 weeks for their 
first scheduled PT appointment had a DNA rate of **8.4%** between April 2025 and June 
2026. For patients that had waited between 36 to 52 weeks, the first contact DNA 
rate was almost double at **15.9%**, falling slightly to **15.8%** for patients that had 
waited over 52 weeks for a first contact appointment. The figures suggest that the timely
offer of contact with a clinician after referral may be important in a 
patient’s engagement with a service.     
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact DNA rate by length of wait in weeks for first contact appointment, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_wait_length_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_wait_length_PT.png"))

```       
    
### PT first contact DNA rate by geographic region
There is a suggestion that, since September 2025, the first contact DNA rate has become
fairly consistent across health board regions, which is likely due to the improving 
data quality in CAPTND. For PT, first contact DNA rates have typically been highest 
across the West and North health board regions at around 10.0%. Rates across the East,
having been notably lower for the first 5 months of the publication period, have since
increased and are now more comparable to the other health board regions. This change 
in trend is likely attributable to modifications in methodologies used to extract data
at board level in NHS Lothian, rather than a true increase in patient DNAs (Figure )
(see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information).

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by health board region, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_hb_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_hb_region_PT.png"))

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. Variability in first contact DNA rates in the East region between `r date_label_5qt` and `r date_label` are likely due to NHS Lothian updating the report used for data extraction at board level. 
<br> 

## CAPTND quality assurance

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Table 3: Difference between raw count of submitted appointment and optimised count of appointments in PT by health board, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>
```{r Total appt comparison, ft.align = "left"}

flextable(table_data6, theme_fun = NULL) %>%
  my_ft_format() %>%
  flextable::bold(i = nrow(table_data6)) %>%
  align(j = 2:4, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
  height(height = 0.236, part = "body") %>%
  hrule(rule = "atleast", part = "body") %>%
  width(j = 1, width = 4, unit = "cm") %>%
  width(j = 2:4, width = 3, unit = "cm")
  #set_caption(caption = paste0("Quarter ending ", date_label), align_with_table = TRUE)

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. \ \ \ CAPTND extract `r data_analysis_latest_date`
    
<br>        
    
    
\newpage
# Glossary  

**Psychological Therapies**  
Psychological Therapies refer to a range of interventions, based on psychological
concepts and theory, which are designed to help people understand and make changes
to their thinking, behaviour, and relationships in order to relieve distress and
to improve functioning. The standard applies specifically to psychological therapies
for treatment of a mental illness or disorder.

**Key variables**  
The combination of identifying variables - including 'UCPN' and 'CHI'/'UPI' -
allowing a patient pathway to be created, which enables further analysis in CAPTND.

**Mean**  
The arithmetical average value i.e. total of a number of values / the total number of 
values.

**Median**  
The middle value when the data values in a dataset are ranked in increasing (or 
decreasing) order of magnitude.

**Total appointments**  
The total number of appointments recorded in CAPTND, whether attended or not. An 
individual can have more than one appointment on a given day.

**First contact appointment**  
The first appointment date that appears in an individual's care pathway.

**DNA Rate**  
Did not attend (DNA) refers to appointments in which a patient did not attend and
gave no prior warning. The DNA rate is the proportion of appointments for which
this occurs out of all appointments in a given period. If for first contact appointments only this
is referred to as the *first contact DNA rate*.

**Region**  
The health boards that make up NHS Scotland can be divided into three geographical
regions:  
East region: *Borders, Fife, Lothian*  
North region: *Highland, Grampian, Orkney, Tayside, Western Isles, Shetland*  
West region: *Ayrshire & Arran, Greater Glasgow & Clyde, Lanarkshire, Forth Valley, Dumfries & Galloway*  

**Official statistics/Official statistics in development:**  
[Official statistics](https://code.statisticsauthority.gov.uk/glossary/#pid-official-statistics)
are statistics produced on behalf of the UK government, while [official statistics in development](https://code.statisticsauthority.gov.uk/glossary/#pid-official-statistics-in-development)
are official statistics which are undergoing a development, and are under testing
by users for quality, trustworthiness, and value.

\newpage

# Contact
**Alan Coventry, Senior information analyst**  
Public Health Scotland  
[phs.captnd@phs.scot](mailto:phs.captnd@phs.scot)

**Luke Taylor, Senior information analyst**  
Public Health Scotland  
[phs.captnd@phs.scot](mailto:phs.captnd@phs.scot)

For all media enquiries please email [phs.comms@phs.scot](mailto:phs.comms@phs.scot) or call 0131 275 6105.

# Further Information
Further information and data for this publication are available from the [publication page](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/psychological-therapies-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-3-march-2026/) on our website.

The next release of this publication will be `r next_pub_date`.

# Rate this publication
Let us know what you think about this publication via. the link at the bottom of this [publication page](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/psychological-therapies-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-3-march-2026/) on the PHS website.

\newpage

# Appendices
## Appendix 1 - Background information

In 2019, an official letter was shared with all NHS Chief Executives and Chief
Officers. This letter stated that the focus on Mental Health (MH) continues to grow.
This brings with it increased demand for services, and a need for a robust evidence
base to support health boards and integrated joint boards (IJBs), where children’s
services are integrated, in effective planning and monitoring.  

One key component of the evidence base is the information health boards gather on adults,
children and young people who receive treatment through CAMHS or PT services. In
order to improve these data, the Scottish Government commissioned ISD (now PHS) to
work with boards to develop an individual level core dataset (CAPTND).
CAPTND data are still at an experimental stage therefore any inferences or
conclusions from this analysis must be treated with caution.  

\newpage

## Appendix 2 - Publication metadata

**Publication title**  
Child, Adolescent, and Psychological Therapies National Dataset (CAPTND):  
Psychological Therapies (PT) Appointment 'Did Not Attend' (DNA) Report (April 2025 - June 2026)

**Description**  
Total and first contact appointment DNAs in Psychological Therapies services
recorded in the Child, Adolescent, and Psychological Therapies National Dataset
(CAPTND), at NHS board and Scotland-level.

**Theme**  
Health and Social Care

**Topic**  
Mental health

**Format**  
Word document

**Data source(s)**  
Child, Adolescent, and Psychological Therapies National Dataset (CAPTND), 
Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation (SIMD), 
Scottish Government Urban Rural Classification.

**Date that data are acquired**  
`r format(as.Date(data_analysis_latest_date), format = "%dth of %B %Y")`

**Release date**  
`r pub_date`

**Frequency**  
6 months

**Timeframe of data and timeliness**  
Data from `r date_label_5qt` to `r date_label`. Biannual release.

**Continuity of data**  
Information has been collected nationally since June 2019.

**Concepts and definitions**  
See the glossary and variables used sections of the report.

**Relevance and key uses of the statistics**  
Data offer detail around individuals accessing mental health services in Scotland.
However, data are still in development so uses are limited given inferences must be
made with caution.

**Accuracy**  
Data are still in development. Data quality investigations to improve accuracy are
ongoing.

**Completeness**  
Data are still in development and many data items in the dataset cannot be provided.
In some cases, missing data items can be inferred from other variables relating
to the same individual - e.g. date of birth can be gleaned from the CHI number -
which has been used where appropriate to achieve maximum completeness of data.

**Comparability**  
Comparisons are discouraged. There will be differences in the measures used and
collection methods compared with PT waiting times statistics, as
well as differences in service structures between the administrations. Users must
carefully read the publications.

**Accessibility**  
It is the policy of Public Health Scotland to make its web sites and products
accessible according to published guidelines. More information on accessibility
can be found on the [PHS website](https://publichealthscotland.scot/accessibility/).

**Value type and unit of measurement**  
Number and percentage of patients by NHS board.

**Disclosure**  
The PHS protocol on Statistical Disclosure Protocol is followed.

**Official statistics accreditation**  
Official statistics in development

**Last published**  
`r prev_pub_date`

**Next published**  
`r next_pub_date`

**Date of first publication**  
1st of June 2021

**Help email**  
[phs.captnd@phs.scot](mailto:phs.captnd@phs.scot)
<br>

**Date form completed**  
`r format(Sys.time(), "%dth of %B %Y")`

\newpage

## Appendix 3 - Early access details

### Pre-release access

Under terms of the 'Pre-release Access to Official Statistics (Scotland) Order 2008',
PHS is obliged to publish information on those receiving pre-release access ('pre-release
access' refers to statistics in their final form prior to publication). The standard
maximum pre-release access is five working days. Shown below are details of those
receiving standard pre-release access.

### Standard pre-release access:

Scottish Government Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC)  
NHS board chief executives  
NHS board communication leads  

### Early access for quality assurance

These statistics will also have been made available to those who needed access to
help quality assure the publication.

\newpage

## Appendix 4 – PHS and official statistics

### About Public Health Scotland (PHS)

PHS is a knowledge-based and intelligence driven organisation with a critical
reliance on data and information to enable it to be an independent voice for the
public's health, leading collaboratively and effectively across the Scottish public
health system, accountable at local and national levels, and providing leadership
and focus for achieving better health and wellbeing outcomes for the population.
Our statistics comply with the [Code of Practice for Statistics](https://www.statisticsauthority.gov.uk/code-of-practice/the-code/)
in terms of trustworthiness, high quality and public value. This also means that
we keep data secure at all stages, through collection, processing, analysis and
output production, and adhere to the Office for National Statistics '[Five Safes](https://blog.ons.gov.uk/2017/01/27/the-five-safes-data-privacy-at-ons/)'
of data privacy.

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<Div custom-style = "*Other formats textbox (only for RMarkdown use)">
Translations and other formats are available on request at:  
phs.otherformats@phs.scot or 0131 314 5300.

This publication is licensed for re-use under the [Open Government Licence v3.0](http://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/doc/open-government-licence/). For more information, visit [www.publichealthscotland.scot/ogl](http://www.publichealthscotland.scot/ogl)
</Div>
